- [Owner sleeps during long runs](owner-sleeps-during-long-runs.md) — silence is usually sleep, never consent; keep state durable, not in context.
- [RESUME HERE](RESUME-HERE.md) — READ FIRST: harness merged to main (#7 done, 1246f87); board (#1) + index-race (#20) are next.
- [Agreeableness is my failure mode](agreeableness-is-my-failure-mode.md) — REJECT and reversal are SUCCESS outcomes; report them without hedging, and beware self-flagellation as the same bias inverted.
- [Swirl and churn: escalate, don't iterate](swirl-and-churn-escalate-dont-iterate.md) — I cannot detect my own churn; the trigger must be mechanical and reviewer-set.
- [Prose is the wrong instrument for formats](prose-is-the-wrong-instrument-for-formats.md) — match instrument to artifact; derive designs from the laws FIRST.
- [Missing workflow is a failure class](missing-workflow-is-a-failure-class.md) — when one rung fails repeatedly, suspect a missing artifact before the worker.
- [Verdicts land verbatim, never retyped](verdicts-land-verbatim-not-retyped.md) — extract mechanically, verify, fault-inject.
- [Guard biases structurally, not by willpower](guard-biases-structurally.md) — the owner guards my biases; that is a single point of failure, so convert each guard into a mechanism.
- [Reading is not verifying](reading-is-not-verifying.md) — behavioural claims must be RUN and quoted from observation; reading only verifies structure.
- [Model topology (owner decision)](model-topology-owner-decision.md) — conductor Fable, gates Opus, cars Sonnet; Car 1 is the probe; never change the mix silently.
- [Truth is constructed by probes](truth-is-constructed-by-probes.md) — no SDK headers here; probe before assuming, and land every probe as a durable artifact; each layer's landed probes are the next layer's headers.
- [Portability requirement + python lean](portability-requirement-and-python-lean.md) — OS portability is standing (issue #14); runtime decision deferred to yard-board design; owner leans python, wants contradictions surfaced.
- [PR requires owner ping FIRST](pr-requires-owner-ping-first.md) — push-notify when READY to open a PR, wait for the go; never open then announce.
- [Entire checkpoint branch retrieval](entire-checkpoint-branch-retrieval.md) — past transcripts live on entire/checkpoints/v1; `git grep` the branch to find them (the `entire ... search` index lags).
- [Bash/pwsh boundary discipline](bash-pwsh-boundary-discipline.md) — run pwsh work through the PowerShell tool, not Bash heredocs; the boundary mangles backticks and paths (approved Retro #2).
- [Mocks are direction, never contract](mocks-are-direction-never-contract.md) — "the track signal, not the track"; contracts are only what passed gates; promotion = writing into a reviewed doc.
- [NIRTS house vocabulary](nirts-house-vocabulary.md) — Need It Right This Second, YAGNI's demand-side twin; the owner's word for "exactly enough."
- [Ask, don't infer, on owner vocabulary](ask-dont-infer-owner-vocabulary.md) — standing rule: unknown owner terms get a clarifying question, never a guess; the lexicon is large.

input.command:
cd C:\Users\Chris\git\starcar
$b1 = @'
Producer hook silently wrote NO dispatched record for 2 of 3 dispatches (invisible, not overdue)

OBSERVED 2026-07-23 (session e92f5a0b), found by hand during forensics on a killed session, NOT by any instrument.

Three dispatches were made this session. Only one landed a `dispatched` record:
- Car 26 `a3bfb5c078bd8da96` -> artifacts/.../dispatched-20260723T204354Z.json, auto-committed as 0667521. GOOD.
- Car 31 `addbb80a3b64cbf2e` (~16:38) -> NO RECORD.
- Car 31's Opus reviewer `ad2b8215ab13183c4` (~16:42) -> NO RECORD.

`git status` was clean throughout, so the two records were never WRITTEN. That is distinct from the other known failure mode (producer writes, record sits untracked and unnoticed - see 1182e09, caught a day late by an owner-ordered sweep).

Not a chronic fault: all day prior the producer was regular, 20 dispatched records between 10:14 and 15:00 with matching `harness:` auto-commits.

WHY THIS IS STRUCTURAL, NOT COSMETIC
The harness detects a missing artifact by finding a `dispatched` record with no matching `returned` one (overdue / presumed-lost). A dispatch that never writes its `dispatched` record is therefore not overdue - it is INVISIBLE. The detector cannot see its own producer's silence. This is absence-blindness one layer below where the design placed its guard, and it is blind in exactly the scenario the mechanism exists for: a session that dies mid-flight leaving no other trace.

ROOT CAUSE NOT ESTABLISHED. Hypothesis only, explicitly UNPROVEN and not to be repeated as fact: git index contention, since the conductor ran 37 `git worktree remove` plus 11 `git branch -d` immediately after the Car 31 dispatch, while the hook auto-commits. The reviewer dispatch had no such concurrent git activity, which weakens that hypothesis and is itself a reason to probe rather than assume.

WHAT WOULD CLOSE THIS
1. PROBE FIRST (behavioural claim - must be RUN, never reasoned): instrument `scripts/Produce-Artifact.ps1` / the launch hook to record every invocation and its exit path, then dispatch repeatedly under concurrent git load and observe. Reading the script cannot settle this.
2. Then a mechanism, not vigilance: the hook is currently "NON-FATAL BY DESIGN" (exit 0 when pwsh is missing), which is correct for not breaking a dispatch but means every other silent failure is also swallowed. A dispatch-count reconcile (conductor-observed dispatches vs records landed) would make the silence loud.
3. Consider whether the detector can ever see this class, or whether a second independent witness is required by construction.

Provenance: docs/friction-log.md, 2026-07-23 evening rows.
'@
gh issue create --title "Producer hook silently wrote NO dispatched record for 2 of 3 dispatches (invisible, not overdue)" --body $b1 --label "area:tooling" | Tee-Object -Variable url1
gh project item-add 6 --owner polecatspeaks --url $url1 | Out-Null; Write-Host "added to board"

input.command:
cd C:\Users\Chris\git\starcar
$b2 = @'
OWNER QUESTION, 2026-07-23: "since we're doing web based for starcar... do we need a framework like playwright?"

Filed under never-drop rather than answered in conversation. Retro rule 5 check done FIRST: `docs/setup.md`'s trigger-gated table has NO browser/visual-testing row, and the repo has zero mentions of playwright/puppeteer/jsdom/headless. So this is a genuine gap, not a deliberate deferral being misread as one.

THE EVIDENCE THAT THE TRIGGER HAS ALREADY FIRED

1. Issue #31 (the board rendering every row needs-attention red) passed ALL 50 node tests. The view model was provably correct - per-item registers all resolved nominal against the real captured snapshot. The defect lived entirely in the CSS cascade: `.register-nominal` set `border-left-color` but not `color`, so nominal children inherited a hot lane's color, and `.car-chip`/`.signal`/`.solari-row` draw their edge with `currentColor`. `board/web/test/minidom.js` is a hand-rolled DOM shim with NO CSS engine, so the entire class of computed-style/cascade defects is structurally invisible to the current suite. It was caught by a human eye at first light.

2. #31's own fix does not guard #31's class. The pin (`board/web/test/register-css.test.js`) is a REGEX OVER THE STYLESHEET'S TEXT. It asserts each `.register-*` rule declares both `border-left-color` and `color`. That pins the file's content; it proves nothing about a rendered pixel. Add `.lane { color: red }` tomorrow and the regex test stays green while the board goes all-red again. A guard that cannot fail for the defect it was written for is the decorative-guard class this repo already has a scar for (branch protection, `enforce_admins: false`).

3. The near-term backlog is almost entirely visual: #30 (conditions grouping / severity calibration, explicitly a HAVAGLANCE question), #29 (IDLE is a state), #12 (car health bar). "Does it render right" is about to be the binding question repeatedly, and we currently have no mechanical way to answer it.

4. `CLAUDE.md` names screenshot drift as one of two surfaces left unguarded even after CI lands, and calls it "both the likeliest and the most embarrassing rot this repo can ship" for a status-board project. A browser driver regenerates `docs/screenshots/` mechanically.

5. FULL SENTENCE FLOW currently ends at a human. The register value crosses store record -> Go assembler -> wire vocabularies -> SSE -> browser JS -> CSS rule -> what an eye sees. Every hop but the last is machine-checked. `getComputedStyle(el).color` makes the last hop empirical - which is the probe doctrine's own rule, since a cascade outcome is a BEHAVIOURAL claim and the doctrine says behavioural claims are settled only by RUNNING.

WHY A REAL BROWSER SPECIFICALLY, NOT A LIGHTER SHIM
jsdom and happy-dom have incomplete cascade and computed-style support. For this exact class they would be a LYING instrument, and this shop's severity philosophy holds that an instrument that cries wolf (or falsely reassures) is worse than none. That disqualifies them on doctrine, not on taste. A real engine is the only honest instrument for a cascade question.

WHAT WE ALREADY HAVE, AND WHY IT IS NOT ENOUGH
The Claude-in-Chrome browser tools are already listed in `docs/setup.md` as ready-now, and are how first light happened. But they are conductor-driven, not runnable in CI, and produce a perishable observation. The probe doctrine is explicit: a probe becomes substrate only when it LANDS as a pinned test. We have the probe half and not the landing half. Chrome tools = the drill; playwright = the pinned red.

COSTS, HONESTLY
- Browser binaries are hundreds of MB; CI gets slower and needs a cache step.
- It would be the first npm-installed dev dependency in a view that is deliberately dependency-free. Precedent exists and points the right way: `docs/setup.md` already reasons that D19's no-build-step rule "binds the BROWSER, never Node - Node is what RUNS the tests". A test-toolchain dependency is not a shipped dependency.
- Cross-platform is fine (issue #14 portability, ubuntu CI leg) but must be verified, not assumed.

RIGHT-SIZED FIRST BITE (NIRTS, not a suite)
ONE spec: serve the real board from the real store, assert `getComputedStyle` colour for a nominal row inside a hot lane (the exact #31 defect, as a real red first), plus one assertion per register, plus mechanical regeneration of the first-light screenshot. Not a broad E2E suite, not page-object scaffolding.

BEFORE BUILDING: ASK FOR THE PRIOR ART. Does the ancestor shop have a browser/visual test pattern to port? The scar for not asking was five design rounds rediscovering written-down practice. The question costs one sentence and belongs to the owner.

DECISION OWNER: the owner (CI minutes, machine config, dependency posture). Trigger stated: the next visual ticket (#30 or #29) is the natural moment.
'@
gh issue create --title "Tooling decision: adopt a real-browser test driver (playwright) for computed-style + screenshot guarding" --body $b2 --label "area:tooling" | Tee-Object -Variable url2
gh project item-add 6 --owner polecatspeaks --url $url2 | Out-Null; Write-Host "added to board"

input.command:
cd C:\Users\Chris\git\starcar
$b3 = @'
OWNER PRACTICE, named 2026-07-23: "I took that as a good chance to run another unannounced surprise drill. Chaos monkey the process."

#21 already covers the DRILL SERIES for conductor handoff: gentle, baseline, then adversarial - all PLANNED. This issue names the complementary practice the owner just exercised: unannounced, unplanned disruption, including converting an accident of the universe into a deliberate test.

WHAT HAPPENED (the first instance, and it was not planned by anyone)
The owner hit a monthly credit limit. Session `67913b32` died mid-work at 16:21:48 (git reflog on `car/31-register-css`), having cut a branch and worktree for issue #31 and written no checkpoint. The owner declined to smooth this over and instead let the next session ("pick up where we left off") run cold as a surprise drill.

WHAT IT CAUGHT - the value case for making this standing
1. The conductor invented a benign story for the debris ("a prior session prepared this and the checkpoint forgot to mention it") that fit the evidence and permitted work to continue, when the truth was a death. Logged, then corrected in the open.
2. The conductor improvised past a VISIBLE contradiction: RESUME-HERE said the #21 drill was "ARMED" for next session while commit 38b67c8's subject says it already PASSED. A checkpoint describing a future that had already happened is a stale document - a lying canary by this repo's own doctrine - and it was readable in the first two tool calls. `CLAUDE.md` lists improvising past a contradiction among the only REAL failures.
3. It surfaced #32 (the producer hook silently skipping 2 of 3 dispatches), which no instrument had reported and which is blind precisely in the killed-session case.

None of these were reachable by the planned drill, because the planned drill grades a GRACEFUL handoff - the easy case, and the one the process was designed for.

THE STRUCTURAL LESSON ALREADY APPLIED
A checkpoint written only at session end does not exist when the end is not graceful. The working-state memory is now written CONTINUOUSLY, on state change - especially on dispatch - and carries an explicit IN FLIGHT section naming live agents, their worktrees, frozen SHAs, and how to re-dispatch. This is the repo's own living-contracts rule ("any commit that changes mutable state updates the ledger in the same commit") generalised from files to session state.

WHAT THIS ISSUE IS FOR - open questions, owner's call
- Should chaos-monkey drills become deliberate and periodic (kill a session mid-train on purpose), or stay opportunistic (convert real disruptions into drills, which costs nothing extra)?
- What else should be chaos-tested? Candidates with real blast radius: a car killed mid-commit; the producer hook disabled; a worktree deleted underneath a running car; CI unavailable at merge time; the artifact store made read-only.
- What is the GRADING RUBRIC? #21's rotation-drill rubric compares a fresh actor's conclusions against what continuation would have found. The analogue here: after a kill, can the next session reconstruct state from durable artifacts alone, and how much was lost? That number is the score.
- Anti-gaming guard applies: a drill counts only if something LANDED - a corrected record, a filed defect, a changed mechanism. This one produced #32, #33, two corrected friction rows and a rewritten checkpoint format.

Related: #21 (planned drill series). Provenance: docs/friction-log.md 2026-07-23 evening rows.
'@
gh issue create --title "Chaos-monkey drills: unannounced disruption as standing practice (complement to the planned #21 series)" --body $b3 --label "area:process" | Tee-Object -Variable url3
gh project item-add 6 --owner polecatspeaks --url $url3 | Out-Null; Write-Host "added to board"

**0 Major, 2 Minor, 3 Notes.** The fix is correct, the guard was watched to fire, and I re-derived the diagnosis independently rather than from the report. Neither Minor is a defect in the shipped behavior; both are recorded with empirical evidence and a named remedy.

## Base and scope (re-derived)

| Worktree at end of review | `git status --porcelain` **empty**, `git diff HEAD` **empty**, `board/web/css/board.css` SHA256 `DC8F2B68…15FBA` identical to review start |

I never mutated the worktree. All fault injection ran against a byte-identical copy in scratch (proved identical over all 34 files by per-file hash before use, and deleted after).

---

## 1. RED evidence — re-derived, not read

Reverted the one added line on the copy and ran the new test:

```
✖ .register-nominal is color-self-contained: declares BOTH border-left-color and color...
  AssertionError [ERR_ASSERTION]: .register-nominal must declare its own color (not just
  border-left-color) - otherwise this register's elements inherit color from an ancestor
  running a different register (issue #31)
    actual: '\n  border-left-color: var(--nominal);\n',
    expected: /(?<!-)\bcolor\s*:/,
ℹ tests 6  ℹ pass 5  ℹ fail 1
```

Verbatim match to the car's reported red, including `actual`. Fails for **the stated reason** (the assertion that pins the missing property), not incidentally: the sibling `rule exists` test stayed green, and the other two registers stayed green.

## 2. Non-vacuity — seven attacks, all on a copy

| Attack | Result |
|---|---|
| A: `board.css` emptied | 0 pass / **6 fail** |
| B: `.register-nominal` renamed `.register-calm` | 4 pass / **2 fail** |
| C: `color` replaced by `background-color` | 5 pass / **1 fail** (lookbehind guard holds) |
| D: replaced by `-webkit-text-fill-color` | 5 pass / **1 fail** (guard holds) |
| E: `border-left-color` removed | 5 pass / **1 fail** |
| G: 4th register `'bagged'` added to `compose.js` REGISTER_ORDER | test count **6 → 8**, `.register-bagged` fails both |
| Restore | 6 pass / 0 fail |

Attack G settles Law 7 empirically: the taxonomy is genuinely **imported**, not hardcoded — adding a register to `board/web/js/compose.js:13` grows the suite and reds on the unbacked class. The `(?<!-)\bcolor\s*:` lookbehind at `register-css.test.js:52` does the work claimed for it.

## 3. GREEN evidence — run myself, observed counts

- `node --test` from `board/web` at `9c4730a`: **tests 56, pass 56, fail 0** (re-run at end of review, same).
- Baseline at `38b67c8` (full-tree `git archive` into scratch): **tests 50, pass 50, fail 0**. The commit message's "50 baseline + 6 new" is TRUE. *(My first baseline attempt archived only `board/web` and showed 1 fail — `sse-event-name.test.js` reads `schema/` from the repo root. My extraction artifact, not a real baseline red. Disclosed so the number is not read as a finding.)*
- `& 'C:\Program Files\Go\bin\go.exe' test ./...` from `board/`: **5/5 packages ok** (`board`, `assemble`, `fold`, `server`, `store`).

## 4. THE FIX'S CORRECTNESS — independently derived

**Hypothesis (a) is genuinely dead.** I compared the fixture's wire `vocabularies` against `schema/vocab/board-defs.json` field by field: `positions`, `outcomes`, `roles`, `liveness` all **byte-equal**. Every id the fixture data actually uses (`returned`; `APPROVE`, `done`; `car`, `reviewer`; `live`, `bagged`, `dark`) is recognised — **zero detector misses**. Building the view model through `buildBoardViewModel` gives a per-item register tally of **`{"nominal": 71}`** — every dispatch, car state, and car outcome resolves nominal. The JS is not the bug.

The three live lanes are `needs-attention` because freshness is `{"kind":"stale","ageBucketMs":555000}` (`compose.js:41`) — honest, and the territory of #29.

**The cascade, measured.** I rendered the real snapshot through `dom-writer.js` into `minidom.js` and computed each text-bearing element's effective `color` by walking ancestors against a model of all 11 `color:` declarations in `board.css` (I verified mechanically that my model captured every one: `grep -n "^\s*color:"` returns exactly lines 35, 61, 68, 73, 121, 130, 134, 138, 189, 215, 221).

216 text-bearing elements. **142 moved red → paper**: 110 `.solari-subject`/`.solari-state`, 24 `.car-subject`/`.car-role`/`.car-state`, 8 `.car-outcome`. That is exactly issue #31's named symptom — "every dispatch row, train chip, and outcome".

Answering each sub-question the brief posed:

- **(i) `.car-chip` / `.signal` / `.solari-row`** — FIXED. The mechanism matters and the commit message states it correctly: at equal `0,1,0` specificity, `board.css:165 / :176 / :204` (`border-left: Npx solid currentColor`) come **after** the register rules at `:128-139`, so the shorthand's `border-left-color: currentColor` **overrides** the register rule's own `border-left-color`. For those three element types the register EDGE is painted entirely from `color`. With `.register-nominal` declaring no `color`, a nominal row inside a hot lane inherited red and drew a red edge. That is the defect, and `color: var(--nominal)` is the minimal correct cure. The gates lane was empty in the fixture, so I injected two synthetic gates and observed `.signal-name`/`.signal-outcome`/`.signal-at` go `RED@138 → PAPER@130`.
- **(ii) `.car-outcome` inside a chip** — FIXED, and **severity is not flattened**. I injected `outcome: 'error'` on one car: post-fix its `.car-outcome.register-needs-attention` stays `RED@138` inside a `PAPER@130` chip, while the seven nominal outcomes go paper. The fix repairs the calm case without muting the hot one.
- **(iii) `.board-condition` in the chrome** — unchanged and correct: 54 rows render `RED@138` from their own `register-needs-attention`, which is what the server actually raised (`record-unrecognised-fields`). Honest red, not this diff's business (and their repetition is #30).
- **(iv) elements carrying NO register class inside a hot lane** — **still inherit red, and I say so plainly.** Measured residue: `.lane-title` ×3, `.lane-primary` ×3, `.track-title` ×1, plus `.declared-not-observed` (absent from this fixture; I injected one and observed `RED@138`). `.lane-secondary` (`:121`) and `.yard-inventory-count` (`:189`) are immune — they set `--text-dim`.

**Ruling on closure:** issue #31 as written is **closed**. Its text names "every dispatch row, train chip, and outcome … values whose registers are PINNED nominal in board-defs.json (returned, done, APPROVE)" — all 142 of those, and nothing else. The residue is lane-level, and the lane genuinely IS hot; the mockup brief at `docs/design/2026-07-23-ui-mockup-brief.md:46` says "A lane's color is the MOST SEVERE of its contributing facts", so a red lane header is the design, not the bug. `.track-title` and `.declared-not-observed` are the one genuinely open edge (Note 1) — item-level labels wearing the lane's register — but they are neither dispatch rows nor chips nor outcomes, and fixing them means picking a color the owner reserved. Not a blocker.

Hop 10 is the one that was broken, and hop 10 is the only one the diff touches. **The `--nominal` == `--text` claim is TRUE**: `board.css:20` `--text: #d9d5c9`, `board.css:23` `--nominal: #d9d5c9` — so the fix is visually a no-op on calm boards and only removes an inheritance path.

# Findings

### Minor 1 — the guard is weaker than its own header comment claims (`board/web/test/register-css.test.js:7-10, :50-56`)

Three declarations that **re-create the exact #31 defect** pass the guard 6/6 (observed on a copy):

| Injection into `.register-nominal` | Suite |
|---|---|

| `color: currentColor;` | **6 pass / 0 fail** |

The file's own comment states the property as *"it declares its own `color` … so no register can ever inherit another's"* (`register-css.test.js:8-10`) — case 1 satisfies the test while the register **does** inherit another's. Related same-class weakness: `findRuleBlock`'s `[^}]*` extraction (`register-css.test.js:38`) is comment-blind, so a `}` inside a comment inside a register block would truncate the extracted block.

Not Major: the guard demonstrably fires on the actual regression class (declaration deleted, wrong property, rule renamed, register added without a CSS rule — attacks A/B/C/D/E/G all red), and no realistic edit produces `color: inherit` on a color-defining register class. Remedy is one line — exclude `inherit|currentColor` and strip `/* … */` before extraction.

The car declined to re-run `analyze`, reasoning "a CSS/test-only change adds no new symbols." That premise is **wrong on both changed paths**:

- `.gitnexus/meta.json` `fileHashes` contains **`board/web/css/board.css`** — the CSS file is indexed, and its hash changed.

The **true** reason the car could not comply: worktrees carry no index. `ls -a` in this review worktree shows **no `.gitnexus` directory**, and `.gitnexus/meta.json` `repoPath = C:\Users\Chris\git\starcar` — running `analyze` would mean writing the shared checkout's index, which every car brief forbids. The action was correct and structurally forced; the justification given would, if reused as precedent, license future cars to skip a `CLAUDE.md`-mandated step on a false premise ("CSS/test-only ⇒ no symbols"). **Conductor action:** re-run `node .gitnexus/run.cjs analyze` on the shared checkout after merge (the index is already behind at `lastCommit = 1182e09`, one commit before this car's base).

Post-fix, 61 text-bearing elements still render red: 54 `.board-condition` (**correct** — their own `register-needs-attention` from the server), 3 `.lane-title` + 3 `.lane-primary` (**design-consistent** — mockup brief `:46`, the lane is genuinely stale), and 1 `.track-title` (`dom-writer.js:114`, no CSS rule declaring color at `board.css:149-152`). `.declared-not-observed` (`dom-writer.js:133`) has **no CSS rule at all** (`grep` confirms) and renders red in a hot lane — I injected one and observed `RED@138`. These two are item-level labels wearing the lane's register, i.e. a thin residue of the issue's ruled-out hypothesis (c). Cheap follow-up ticket (a `color` on `.track-title` / `.declared-not-observed`); correctly deferred here because it picks a visual decision reserved to the owner alongside #29.

All six agree today (table above). Before this commit, **zero** edges were mechanically pinned; `register-css.test.js` now pins CSS ↔ `compose.js`. Still unpinned and pre-existing: `compose.js:13` ↔ `$defs.register.enum`, and `boarddefs.go:29` ↔ `$defs.register.enum` (`grep` finds no test asserting either — contrast `sse-event-name.test.js`, which pins `SSE_EVENT_NAME` against the schema and is the exemplar to copy). Law 6 exposure **reduced** by this diff, not created. Worth a ticket, not a finding.

### Note 3 — `board.css:126` is loose but not this diff's debt

`/* the three registers, and NOTHING else colors an element */` sits above a file with 10 class rules that declare `color` (`:61, :68, :73, :121, :130, :134, :138, :189, :215, :221`). Byte-identical at the parent, so not invalidated by this commit, and `board.css:12-14` supplies the intended narrow reading ("never a fourth shade of alarm"). Flagged only so a future reader does not mistake it for a claim the new test enforces.

content.text:
**Playwright: yes, and the trigger has already fired — but the case is narrower and better-evidenced than "we're web-based, so we need a browser framework."** I filed it as **#33** rather than answering into a chat reply that evaporates, and checked `docs/setup.md`'s trigger-gated table first so I wasn't crying wolf on a deliberate deferral — there's no browser/visual row there, and zero mentions of playwright, jsdom, or headless anywhere in the repo. This is a real gap.

The evidence is #31 itself, and it's sharper than I realized when I dispatched the car. That defect passed **all 50 node tests**. The view model was provably correct — every per-item register resolved nominal against the real snapshot. The bug lived entirely in the CSS cascade, and `board/web/test/minidom.js` is a hand-rolled shim with no CSS engine, so that whole class is structurally invisible to our suite. A human eye caught it.

Worse, and this is the part that decides the question: **#31's own fix does not guard #31's class.** The pin Car 31 wrote is a regex over the stylesheet's *text*. It asserts each `.register-*` rule declares both properties. Add `.lane { color: red }` tomorrow and that test stays green while the board goes all-red again. That's the decorative-guard shape this repo already has a scar for — branch protection with `enforce_admins: false`, which read back perfect and stopped nothing.

On the framework choice: jsdom and happy-dom have incomplete cascade support, which for this exact class makes them a *lying* instrument — disqualified by our own severity philosophy, not by taste. A real engine is the only honest answer to a cascade question. And note we already have the probe half: the Claude-in-Chrome tools are how first light happened. What's missing is the *landing* half — the probe doctrine says an observation becomes substrate only when it lands as a pinned test. Chrome is the drill; Playwright is the red. Right-sized first bite is one spec, not a suite: serve the real board, assert `getComputedStyle` on a nominal row inside a hot lane, regenerate the screenshot mechanically. Before building, the standing question is yours: **does the ancestor shop have a browser-test pattern to port?** Not asking cost five design rounds last time.

**Now the drill, graded honestly. It caught three things, all mine.**

I got the class wrong. I found the orphaned branch and wrote a benign story — "a prior session prepared it and the checkpoint forgot a line" — that fit the evidence and let me keep working. The truth was a death: the reflog pins `car/31-register-css` to 16:21:48, and session `67913b32` never wrote a checkpoint at all. My proposed remedy assumed a graceful close, so it would not have helped. Corrected in the open with a strikethrough, not deleted.

I improvised past a visible contradiction. `RESUME-HERE` said the #21 drill was "ARMED" for next session; commit `38b67c8`'s subject says it already **PASSED**. A checkpoint describing a future that already happened is a stale document — a lying canary by our own doctrine — and both halves were in my first two tool calls. That's on the short list of real failures. It cost nothing today purely by luck.

And the one that matters most: **the producer hook silently skipped 2 of my 3 dispatches.** Car 26 landed a `dispatched` record; Car 31 and its reviewer landed none, and `git status` was clean, so they were never written — distinct from the known write-but-don't-commit mode that bit us at `1182e09`. All day prior it was regular: 20 records, 10:14 to 15:00. This is structural, not cosmetic. The harness finds a missing artifact by spotting a `dispatched` record with no matching `returned` one — so a dispatch that never writes its record isn't *overdue*, it's **invisible**. The mechanism built to make a killed session survivable is blind in exactly the case it exists for. Filed as **#32**. I did *not* assert a root cause; my contention hypothesis is labeled unproven, and it needs a probe, not reasoning.

I also applied the lesson rather than just writing it down: the working-state checkpoint is now rewritten continuously with an explicit IN FLIGHT section naming live agents, worktrees, and frozen SHAs, so another kill costs the re-dispatch and nothing more. **#34** files chaos-monkey drilling as standing practice with the open questions for you — periodic or opportunistic, what else to chaos-test, and what the grading rubric is.

Both dispatches are still running (Car 31's Opus review, Car 26). Nothing has reached `main`. `dev` is pushed at `8f82464` and I'm watching CI to a recorded terminal state. One cost note given what prompted all this: I put a second unit in flight before you mentioned credits — an Opus review plus a Sonnet car. Say the word if you want me to cap concurrency to one unit.

<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result># VERDICT: APPROVE

**0 Major, 2 Minor, 3 Notes.** The fix is correct, the guard was watched to fire, and I re-derived the diagnosis independently rather than from the report. Neither Minor is a defect in the shipped behavior; both are recorded with empirical evidence and a named remedy.

---

## Base and scope (re-derived)

| Check | Observed |
|---|---|
| Worktree HEAD | `9c4730acaa02ce7d75ab7481d4829d916b7ec890`, branch `car/31-register-css` |
| Parent | `38b67c8c0b9f75099aa334a5cf2af340c810920d` — matches the briefed base |
| Lineage | `git merge-base --is-ancestor 38b67c8 dev` → **true**; dev tip `0667521` adds only a harness dispatch record, `git diff --stat 38b67c8..dev -- board/web/css board/web/test` is **empty** (no conflict) |
| Diff | exactly 2 files, +60/−0: `board/web/css/board.css` (+1), `board/web/test/register-css.test.js` (new, 59) |
| Scope discipline | `dom-writer.js`, `render.js`, `compose.js` (freshness/IDLE) **untouched** — confirmed by `git show --stat`. Honored. |
| Worktree at end of review | `git status --porcelain` **empty**, `git diff HEAD` **empty**, `board/web/css/board.css` SHA256 `DC8F2B68…15FBA` identical to review start |

I never mutated the worktree. All fault injection ran against a byte-identical copy in scratch (proved identical over all 34 files by per-file hash before use, and deleted after).

---

## 1. RED evidence — re-derived, not read

Reverted the one added line on the copy and ran the new test:

```
✖ .register-nominal is color-self-contained: declares BOTH border-left-color and color...
  AssertionError [ERR_ASSERTION]: .register-nominal must declare its own color (not just
  border-left-color) - otherwise this register's elements inherit color from an ancestor
  running a different register (issue #31)
    actual: '\n  border-left-color: var(--nominal);\n',
    expected: /(?<!-)\bcolor\s*:/,
ℹ tests 6  ℹ pass 5  ℹ fail 1
```

Verbatim match to the car's reported red, including `actual`. Fails for **the stated reason** (the assertion that pins the missing property), not incidentally: the sibling `rule exists` test stayed green, and the other two registers stayed green.

## 2. Non-vacuity — seven attacks, all on a copy

| Attack | Result |
|---|---|
| A: `board.css` emptied | 0 pass / **6 fail** |
| B: `.register-nominal` renamed `.register-calm` | 4 pass / **2 fail** |
| C: `color` replaced by `background-color` | 5 pass / **1 fail** (lookbehind guard holds) |
| D: replaced by `-webkit-text-fill-color` | 5 pass / **1 fail** (guard holds) |
| E: `border-left-color` removed | 5 pass / **1 fail** |
| G: 4th register `'bagged'` added to `compose.js` REGISTER_ORDER | test count **6 → 8**, `.register-bagged` fails both |
| Restore | 6 pass / 0 fail |

Attack G settles Law 7 empirically: the taxonomy is genuinely **imported**, not hardcoded — adding a register to `board/web/js/compose.js:13` grows the suite and reds on the unbacked class. The `(?<!-)\bcolor\s*:` lookbehind at `register-css.test.js:52` does the work claimed for it.

## 3. GREEN evidence — run myself, observed counts

- `node --test` from `board/web` at `9c4730a`: **tests 56, pass 56, fail 0** (re-run at end of review, same).
- Baseline at `38b67c8` (full-tree `git archive` into scratch): **tests 50, pass 50, fail 0**. The commit message's "50 baseline + 6 new" is TRUE. *(My first baseline attempt archived only `board/web` and showed 1 fail — `sse-event-name.test.js` reads `schema/` from the repo root. My extraction artifact, not a real baseline red. Disclosed so the number is not read as a finding.)*
- `& 'C:\Program Files\Go\bin\go.exe' test ./...` from `board/`: **5/5 packages ok** (`board`, `assemble`, `fold`, `server`, `store`).

## 4. THE FIX'S CORRECTNESS — independently derived

**Hypothesis (a) is genuinely dead.** I compared the fixture's wire `vocabularies` against `schema/vocab/board-defs.json` field by field: `positions`, `outcomes`, `roles`, `liveness` all **byte-equal**. Every id the fixture data actually uses (`returned`; `APPROVE`, `done`; `car`, `reviewer`; `live`, `bagged`, `dark`) is recognised — **zero detector misses**. Building the view model through `buildBoardViewModel` gives a per-item register tally of **`{"nominal": 71}`** — every dispatch, car state, and car outcome resolves nominal. The JS is not the bug.

The three live lanes are `needs-attention` because freshness is `{"kind":"stale","ageBucketMs":555000}` (`compose.js:41`) — honest, and the territory of #29.

**The cascade, measured.** I rendered the real snapshot through `dom-writer.js` into `minidom.js` and computed each text-bearing element's effective `color` by walking ancestors against a model of all 11 `color:` declarations in `board.css` (I verified mechanically that my model captured every one: `grep -n "^\s*color:"` returns exactly lines 35, 61, 68, 73, 121, 130, 134, 138, 189, 215, 221).

216 text-bearing elements. **142 moved red → paper**: 110 `.solari-subject`/`.solari-state`, 24 `.car-subject`/`.car-role`/`.car-state`, 8 `.car-outcome`. That is exactly issue #31's named symptom — "every dispatch row, train chip, and outcome".

Answering each sub-question the brief posed:

- **(i) `.car-chip` / `.signal` / `.solari-row`** — FIXED. The mechanism matters and the commit message states it correctly: at equal `0,1,0` specificity, `board.css:165 / :176 / :204` (`border-left: Npx solid currentColor`) come **after** the register rules at `:128-139`, so the shorthand's `border-left-color: currentColor` **overrides** the register rule's own `border-left-color`. For those three element types the register EDGE is painted entirely from `color`. With `.register-nominal` declaring no `color`, a nominal row inside a hot lane inherited red and drew a red edge. That is the defect, and `color: var(--nominal)` is the minimal correct cure. The gates lane was empty in the fixture, so I injected two synthetic gates and observed `.signal-name`/`.signal-outcome`/`.signal-at` go `RED@138 → PAPER@130`.
- **(ii) `.car-outcome` inside a chip** — FIXED, and **severity is not flattened**. I injected `outcome: 'error'` on one car: post-fix its `.car-outcome.register-needs-attention` stays `RED@138` inside a `PAPER@130` chip, while the seven nominal outcomes go paper. The fix repairs the calm case without muting the hot one.
- **(iii) `.board-condition` in the chrome** — unchanged and correct: 54 rows render `RED@138` from their own `register-needs-attention`, which is what the server actually raised (`record-unrecognised-fields`). Honest red, not this diff's business (and their repetition is #30).
- **(iv) elements carrying NO register class inside a hot lane** — **still inherit red, and I say so plainly.** Measured residue: `.lane-title` ×3, `.lane-primary` ×3, `.track-title` ×1, plus `.declared-not-observed` (absent from this fixture; I injected one and observed `RED@138`). `.lane-secondary` (`:121`) and `.yard-inventory-count` (`:189`) are immune — they set `--text-dim`.

**Ruling on closure:** issue #31 as written is **closed**. Its text names "every dispatch row, train chip, and outcome … values whose registers are PINNED nominal in board-defs.json (returned, done, APPROVE)" — all 142 of those, and nothing else. The residue is lane-level, and the lane genuinely IS hot; the mockup brief at `docs/design/2026-07-23-ui-mockup-brief.md:46` says "A lane's color is the MOST SEVERE of its contributing facts", so a red lane header is the design, not the bug. `.track-title` and `.declared-not-observed` are the one genuinely open edge (Note 1) — item-level labels wearing the lane's register — but they are neither dispatch rows nor chips nor outcomes, and fixing them means picking a color the owner reserved. Not a blocker.

## 5. THE SENTENCE CHECK — register, producer to pixel

Traced for `state: "returned"` on a dispatch row:

| # | Hop | file:line |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | State word minted by the fold's precedence winner | `board/fold/algorithm.go:10`, `:205`; overdue derived at `:240` |
| 2 | Serialized onto the wire item | `board/fold/output.go:16` `"state": d.State` |

# Findings

### Minor 1 — the guard is weaker than its own header comment claims (`board/web/test/register-css.test.js:7-10, :50-56`)

Three declarations that **re-create the exact #31 defect** pass the guard 6/6 (observed on a copy):

| Injection into `.register-nominal` | Suite |
|---|---|
| `color: inherit;` | **6 pass / 0 fail** |
| `color: currentColor;` | **6 pass / 0 fail** |
| `/* color: var(--nominal); */` (commented out) | **6 pass / 0 fail** |

The file's own comment states the property as *"it declares its own `color` … so no register can ever inherit another's"* (`register-css.test.js:8-10`) — case 1 satisfies the test while the register **does** inherit another's. Related same-class weakness: `findRuleBlock`'s `[^}]*` extraction (`register-css.test.js:38`) is comment-blind, so a `}` inside a comment inside a register block would truncate the extracted block.

Not Major: the guard demonstrably fires on the actual regression class (declaration deleted, wrong property, rule renamed, register added without a CSS rule — attacks A/B/C/D/E/G all red), and no realistic edit produces `color: inherit` on a color-defining register class. Remedy is one line — exclude `inherit|currentColor` and strip `/* … */` before extraction.

### Minor 2 — the disclosed GitNexus deviation's REASONING is false (right action, wrong reason)

The car declined to re-run `analyze`, reasoning "a CSS/test-only change adds no new symbols." That premise is **wrong on both changed paths**:

- `.gitnexus/meta.json` `fileHashes` contains **`board/web/css/board.css`** — the CSS file is indexed, and its hash changed.
- `fileHashes` contains **all seven** pre-existing `board/web/test/*.test.js` files, so the test directory is in scope; the new `register-css.test.js` declares a new top-level function `findRuleBlock` (`register-css.test.js:29`) plus six test registrations — new symbols by any reading.

The **true** reason the car could not comply: worktrees carry no index. `ls -a` in this review worktree shows **no `.gitnexus` directory**, and `.gitnexus/meta.json` `repoPath = C:\Users\Chris\git\starcar` — running `analyze` would mean writing the shared checkout's index, which every car brief forbids. The action was correct and structurally forced; the justification given would, if reused as precedent, license future cars to skip a `CLAUDE.md`-mandated step on a false premise ("CSS/test-only ⇒ no symbols"). **Conductor action:** re-run `node .gitnexus/run.cjs analyze` on the shared checkout after merge (the index is already behind at `lastCommit = 1182e09`, one commit before this car's base).

### Note 1 — residual lane-level inheritance, measured and out of this issue's scope

Post-fix, 61 text-bearing elements still render red: 54 `.board-condition` (**correct** — their own `register-needs-attention` from the server), 3 `.lane-title` + 3 `.lane-primary` (**design-consistent** — mockup brief `:46`, the lane is genuinely stale), and 1 `.track-title` (`dom-writer.js:114`, no CSS rule declaring color at `board.css:149-152`). `.declared-not-observed` (`dom-writer.js:133`) has **no CSS rule at all** (`grep` confirms) and renders red in a hot lane — I injected one and observed `RED@138`. These two are item-level labels wearing the lane's register, i.e. a thin residue of the issue's ruled-out hypothesis (c). Cheap follow-up ticket (a `color` on `.track-title` / `.declared-not-observed`); correctly deferred here because it picks a visual decision reserved to the owner alongside #29.

All six agree today (table above). Before this commit, **zero** edges were mechanically pinned; `register-css.test.js` now pins CSS ↔ `compose.js`. Still unpinned and pre-existing: `compose.js:13` ↔ `$defs.register.enum`, and `boarddefs.go:29` ↔ `$defs.register.enum` (`grep` finds no test asserting either — contrast `sse-event-name.test.js`, which pins `SSE_EVENT_NAME` against the schema and is the exemplar to copy). ### Note 3 — `board.css:126` is loose but not this diff's debt

`/* the three registers, and NOTHING else colors an element */` sits above a file with 10 class rules that declare `color` (`:61, :68, :73, :121, :130, :134, :138, :189, :215, :221`). Byte-identical at the parent, so not invalidated by this commit, and `board.css:12-14` supplies the intended narrow reading ("never a fourth shade of alarm"). input.prompt:
You are the adversarial sentence-check reviewer for Car 26 on the StarCar first-light iteration (repo: StarCar, a process-showcase repo; the yard board is a Go server + vanilla-ESM browser view under board/, the artifact store is artifacts/**/*.json written by PowerShell producer scripts under scripts/). Binding REJECT authority: any Major = REJECT; disclosed-but-wrong does not clear review. READ-ONLY: edit nothing, commit nothing, push nothing in any tracked location. Work ONLY in the detached worktree at C:/Users/Chris/AppData/Local/Temp/claude/C--Users-Chris-git-starcar/e92f5a0b-1b9e-4913-98b3-c319b4d5e90b/scratchpad/review-car26 (HEAD must be d0d9d77ef305ff6f13c28e6c48825c01d25b47b8 - verify first with git log -1 --format='%H', STOP if not). NEVER touch the shared checkout at C:/Users/Chris/git/starcar or the car's own worktree. NO NESTED DELEGATION.

Platform notes: Go is NOT on PATH - invoke by full path `C:\Program Files\Go\bin\go.exe`. Pester via pwsh. Node tests from board/web with bare `node --test`. You MAY fault-inject locally to re-derive a red, PROVIDED you work on a byte-identical scratch copy or restore byte-identical afterwards and PROVE it (git status --porcelain empty and git diff HEAD empty at the end, plus a hash comparison).

SCOPE: two commits, base 38b67c8c0b9f75099aa334a5cf2af340c810920d:
- 969e856a2a5441e2feee1e6738440b8a49799e03 (Go mechanism: board/store/store.go, board/store/store_test.go)
- d0d9d77ef305ff6f13c28e6c48825c01d25b47b8 (schema contract: schema/starcar-artifact.schema.json + 4 new files under schema/vectors/)
Total 7 files, +96/-1.

THE ISSUE (#26): the board's first live serve disclosed that store records carry two fields the schema never enumerated - `model` and `body_file` - and the board raised a `record-unrecognised-fields` condition per record. That disclosure is Law 1/Law 4 working as designed. Both fields are OBSERVED PRODUCER FIELDS WITH CLEAR PROVENANCE (`model` written at scripts/Produce-Artifact.ps1:285 guarded to kind=dispatched, sourced from tool_response.resolvedModel read at :204; `body_file` written at scripts/Migrate-Verdicts.ps1:152 on migrated verdicts), so the ruled disposition was to DECLARE them, never to suppress the detector.

VERIFY - re-derive each of the car's claims YOURSELF; the report is not evidence:
1. Base/commit/scope: HEAD, that the diff is exactly those 7 files, and that nothing outside scope was touched. Confirm specifically that the `record-unrecognised-fields` DETECTOR MECHANISM itself (board/store/store.go around :285-299) is untouched and still fires for genuinely unknown fields - fault-inject a record with a junk field and OBSERVE the condition still raised. If the car weakened, filtered, or downgraded the detector, that is an instant Major (Law 1/Law 4: the honest detector must keep firing).
2. RED EVIDENCE, Go side: the car reports `TestScanKnownProducerFieldsNotUnrecognised` failed before the fix with a message naming `model` as unrecognised. Re-derive it (revert the two struct fields on a copy, run, observe). Confirm it fails FOR THE STATED REASON.
3. RED EVIDENCE, schema side: the car reports it found the EXISTING conformance-vector instrument (schema/vectors/*.json + .expect siblings, consumed by scripts/tests/Artifact.Tests.ps1 via Test-StarcarArtifact in scripts/Artifact.psm1:20) rather than inventing a framework, and added two wrong-type vectors that failed before the fix with "Expected $false, but got $true". Re-derive. VERIFY THE PRIOR-ART CLAIM ITSELF: does that instrument really exist at this base and is that really how it is consumed? Open it.
4. NON-VACUITY of both reds. Attack them: does the Go test actually pin the class, or would it pass with only one field declared? Would the schema vectors pass if the type were declared as something else entirely? Does declaring `"type": "string"` actually make a wrong-typed value invalid given the schema's OPEN additionalProperties posture - i.e. is the vector testing what it claims? Try to make each test pass while the defect is present.
5. GREEN, RUN YOURSELF, report OBSERVED counts: `& 'C:\Program Files\Go\bin\go.exe' test ./... -count=1` from board/ (expect 5/5 packages); `node --test` from board/web (expect 50/50, collateral - this diff should not touch it); Pester `scripts/tests` (car claims 249/249 = 247 baseline + 2 new). Also derive the BASELINE at 38b67c8 yourself so "247 baseline + 2 new" is verified arithmetic, not an assertion. NOTE: `scripts/probes` reds with "The term 'sh' is not recognized" in a shell without `sh` on PATH - that is a KNOWN environment gap logged at docs/friction-log.md, NOT a regression; name it as that if you see it, and do not report it as a finding.
6. THE EMPIRICAL CLAIM - the actual point of the change. The car claims 56 `record-unrecognised-fields` conditions before (31 model, 25 body_file) and 0 after, measured against the REAL store at C:/Users/Chris/git/starcar/artifacts. It also discloses that the issue said 52 (27/25) and attributes the difference to the store growing during today's session. RE-DERIVE THE AFTER-COUNT YOURSELF against the real store (read-only - never write to artifacts/). If it is not 0, report the remaining field names verbatim. Independently sanity-check the before/after story: count how many records in the real store actually carry each field, and confirm the arithmetic hangs together. A number that exists is not a number that means what you assumed.
7. TYPE CORRECTNESS: the car chose `string` for both. Verify against the REAL records what types those fields actually hold in practice, and against what the producers actually write (scripts/Produce-Artifact.ps1:285 and scripts/Migrate-Verdicts.ps1:152). A type that is right for today's records but wrong for what the producer can emit is a latent defect - rule on it.

THE SENTENCE CHECK: `model` and `body_file` are cross-boundary values. Trace each producer to final consumer, every hop with file:line: the PowerShell producer that writes it -> the JSON record on disk -> the schema that now declares it -> board/store's double-decode and known-key diff (typedRecord -> buildTypedKeys -> the unknown-field comparison) -> whether it reaches the assembler/wire/board view at all, or stops at the store. State the trace. Check every hand-maintained mirror of the record's field set: typedRecord's json tags, the schema's properties, schema/index-format.md's canonical field order, any fixture or wire sample, and any doc enumerating record fields - and report whether they agree. The car claims index-format.md deliberately excludes both fields from canonical ordering and therefore needed no edit; OPEN IT and rule on whether that claim is true, because a wrong call there is a silent contract drift.

DOC CHECK: the documents this diff plausibly invalidates - the typedRecord comment block in store.go (it explains the known-key set and cites D17/DR3-1; is its story still true with two more fields?), schema/starcar-artifact.schema.json's own $comment blocks, schema/index-format.md, docs/design/2026-07-21-v0-yard-skeleton-design.md (D17/DR3-1), docs/specs/2026-07-23-yard-board-spec.md, docs/contracts/state-ledger.md, docs/contracts/gating-matrix.md, docs/glossary.md, docs/doc-map.md. The car says it grep-checked all of these and found none invalidated. Verify that independently - a stale doc left behind is a MAJOR, and so is a dead citation. Open every file:line the diff's comments and commit messages cite and confirm each is TRUE.

GUARD CHECK: the two new schema vectors and the new Go test are guards. Has anyone WATCHED each fire? Demand and re-derive the fault-injection evidence yourself. A passing-on-arrival test is an assertion, not an observation.

ADJUDICATE these disclosed deviations:
(a) the before-count discrepancy (56 vs the issue's 52) attributed to live store growth - is that explanation TRUE? Verify against the store rather than accepting it.
(b) the decision NOT to add positive-shape vectors, only wrong-type ones.
(c) the decision that schema/index-format.md needed no edit.

CONSTITUTION CHECK: name each law the diff implicates with one line of evidence each. At minimum: Law 1 (the detector must keep firing - a suppressed truth surface is the worst outcome here), Law 4 (silent loss), Law 6 (second copies of the record field set - it now lives in at least typedRecord, the schema, and possibly index-format.md; do they agree and is any edge mechanically pinned?), Law 7 (recognition vocabulary as DATA not schema enums - does declaring these two fields as typed properties violate the open-posture rule the schema's own $comment describes?), and the TDD rule.

CONVERGENCE CHECK: not applicable - round 1, no prior rounds.

VERDICT: APPROVE or REJECT up top; findings by severity (Major/Minor/Note) with file:line each. House rules: REJECT is a SUCCESS outcome, effort is never mitigating, softening a finding produces a spelling check - and equally, do not manufacture a finding to look thorough.

END YOUR REPORT WITH THE ARTIFACT ENVELOPE (mandatory - your verdict is a returned dispatch too): a fenced block, info string starcar-artifact, with three fields - outcome (APPROVE / REJECT / honest-stop), findings, abstract. NO ANGLE BRACKETS anywhere inside the envelope.

input.description: Inspect verdict-landing tooling and CI watch output

content.content:
=== verdict landing tooling ===


=== Land-Verdict param block ===

=== CI watch output so far ===
Name
----
Land-Verdict.ps1
Migrate-Verdicts.ps1
Verify-Verdict.ps1
# Land-Verdict.ps1 -- extract a dispatched agent's verdict VERBATIM from the session
# transcript and land it in artifacts/reviews/ (R10's landing convention, harness #7's
# migration commit - the directory used to be docs/reviews/, retired).
#
# WHY THIS EXISTS (the class, not the instance):
#
# A dispatch's output is ephemeral by default. The verdict lives in a session
# transcript nobody reads, and the only thing standing between an artifact and
# oblivion is the conductor remembering to copy it out -- which is VIGILANCE, the
# weakest tier in the Healing Loop's hierarchy, below even a written procedure.
#
# Entire.io already solves DURABILITY: every session is checkpointed to a public
# branch. What it does not solve is ADDRESSABILITY. A verdict buried in a
# multi-megabyte JSONL is safe and unusable, while README.md promises "review
# verdicts and REJECT records committed in-repo as they happen" -- a promise a
# reader cannot navigate to. Persisted-but-unfindable is not kept.
#
# And the obvious manual fix is worse than the gap. On 2026-07-22 the conductor
# began hand-transcribing a review verdict about its OWN design into what was then
# docs/reviews/ (that directory is retired; verdicts land in artifacts/reviews/ as of
# harness #7's migration commit - this sentence is historical narrative, describing
# where that specific incident actually went, not today's landing target).
# That is a hand-maintained mirror at a process boundary -- the exact scar class in
# CLAUDE.md -- with the aggravating factor that the author being reviewed was doing
# the copying. A softened phrase or a dropped finding would be undetectable.
# VERBATIM-BY-CONSTRUCTION BEATS VERBATIM-BY-DISCIPLINE.
#
# So: this script reads the transcript, the conductor never retypes, and the landed
# file carries a SHA-256 of its own body so the verbatim claim is checkable rather
# than asserted.
#
# TRANSCRIPT SOURCE: the caller supplies -TranscriptPath explicitly (now MANDATORY).
# This is the harness backfill path (spec S4 row 3 [m3]): backfill happens precisely when

# WHY THIS EXISTS (the class, not the instance):
#
# A dispatch's output is ephemeral by default. The verdict lives in a session
# transcript nobody reads, and the only thing standing between an artifact and
# oblivion is the conductor remembering to copy it out -- which is VIGILANCE, the
# weakest tier in the Healing Loop's hierarchy, below even a written procedure.
#
# Entire.io already solves DURABILITY: every session is checkpointed to a public
# branch. What it does not solve is ADDRESSABILITY. A verdict buried in a
# multi-megabyte JSONL is safe and unusable, while README.md promises "review

content.content:
#   scripts/Land-Verdict.ps1 -TaskId <id> -Out artifacts/reviews/<file>.md -TranscriptPath <path> `
#       -Title '...' -Gate '...' -Target '...' -Base <sha> -Verdict 'REJECT' [-Round 2]
#
# Windows PowerShell 5.1 compatible: no ternary, no &&/||, ASCII only.

param(
    [Parameter(Mandatory)] [string]$TaskId,
    [Parameter(Mandatory)] [string]$Out,
    [Parameter(Mandatory)] [string]$Title,
    [Parameter(Mandatory)] [string]$Gate,
    [Parameter(Mandatory)] [string]$Target,
    [Parameter(Mandatory)] [string]$Base,
    [Parameter(Mandatory)] [string]$Verdict,
    [string]$Round = '',
    [string]$Reviewer = 'car agent type, Opus, read-only, detached worktree, no delegation',
    [Parameter(Mandatory)] [string]$TranscriptPath,
    [switch]$Force
)

Set-StrictMode -Version Latest
$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'

# --- refuse to land into the retired docs/reviews/ (M-B guard, fix-cycle round 1) ----
#
# R10's whole claim (docs/setup.md's Verdict-landing row) is that nothing can land
# silently unverified in a resurrected docs/reviews/, because the landing convention,
# Verify-Verdict.ps1's default, and the docs all point at artifacts/reviews/ (Law 6 -
# one location, one owner). Verify-Verdict.ps1's default moving is necessary but not
# sufficient: an operator who still types the old path (muscle memory, a stale
# reference, a copy-pasted command from before the migration) would land a file that
# Verify-Verdict.ps1 never looks at by default - silent, unverified, exactly what R10
# was written to prevent. This check closes that gap at the write path itself, not
# only at the read path.
#
# Narrow by design: it matches "docs/reviews" (or "docs\reviews") as a PATH SEGMENT
# (anchored on / \ or the string boundary), never a bare substring match - a target

merge: Car 31 - #31 register CSS fix, APPROVED (0 Major, 2 Minor, 3 Notes)

The all-red first-light board is fixed. .register-nominal declared only
border-left-color while the other two registers declared both it and color, so a
hot lane's color cascaded into every nominal child - and .car-chip/.signal/
.solari-row draw their register edge with currentColor, so nominal items inherited
the red border too. One line: color: var(--nominal).

Reviewer (Opus, detached worktree at the frozen SHA) re-derived rather than read:
- RED reproduced byte-for-byte on a scratch copy, failing for the stated reason.
- Seven vacuity attacks; six killed the guard. Adding a 4th register to
  compose.js REGISTER_ORDER grew the suite 6 to 8 and reds on the unbacked class,
  proving Law 7 import rather than a hardcoded taxonomy.
- Fix measured, not argued: 142 of 216 text-bearing elements move red to paper
  (110 solari rows, 24 car-chip spans, 8 outcomes) - exactly the issue's named
  symptom. An injected "error" outcome stays RED inside a PAPER chip, so severity
  is not flattened.
- Twelve-hop sentence trace, fold to pixel; hop 10 was the only broken hop and the
  only hop touched. Six taxonomy mirrors compared mechanically, all agree.

Suites observed by the reviewer at 9c4730a: node --test 56/56, go test ./... 5/5
packages, baseline at 38b67c8 re-derived as 50/50 so "50 + 6 new" is verified
arithmetic.

Verdict landed VERBATIM at
artifacts/reviews/2026-07-23-car31-register-css-review-round1-APPROVE.md
(25941 chars, sha256 aef0aeb1...). Minors and Notes filed as tickets, not folded
here: the guard accepts color:inherit (one-line remedy), residual lane-level
inheritance on .track-title/.declared-not-observed, and two still-unpinned edges
of the register taxonomy.

Closes #31.
'@

input.command:
cd C:\Users\Chris\git\starcar
$b = @'
FOUND BY: the Car 31 adversarial reviewer (Opus), round 1, recorded as MINOR-1 in artifacts/reviews/2026-07-23-car31-register-css-review-round1-APPROVE.md. Not a blocker (verdict was APPROVE, 0 Major) - filed rather than folded, per the review calibration.

THE DEFECT IN THE GUARD
`board/web/test/register-css.test.js` pins that every register class in compose.js's REGISTER_ORDER declares BOTH `border-left-color` and `color`, so no register can inherit another's color (issue #31's class). The reviewer fault-injected three declarations that RE-CREATE the exact #31 defect and observed the suite pass 6/6 on each:

- `color: inherit;`      -> 6 pass / 0 fail
- `color: currentColor;` -> 6 pass / 0 fail
- `/* color: var(--nominal); */` (commented out) -> 6 pass / 0 fail

Case 1 is the sharp one: the test file's own comment (register-css.test.js:7-10) states the property as "it declares its own color ... so no register can ever inherit another's" - and `color: inherit` satisfies the assertion while the register DOES inherit another's. The guard's comment claims more than the guard checks.

RELATED, SAME CLASS: `findRuleBlock`'s `[^}]*` extraction (register-css.test.js:38) is comment-blind, so a `}` inside a comment inside a register rule block would truncate the extracted block and could mask a missing declaration.

WHY IT IS NOT MAJOR (the reviewer's reasoning, recorded so a future reader does not re-litigate)
The guard demonstrably fires on every realistic regression. Seven attacks were run; six killed it: stylesheet emptied (0 pass / 6 fail), rule renamed (4/2), `background-color` substituted (5/1), `-webkit-text-fill-color` substituted (5/1), `border-left-color` removed (5/1), and a 4th register added to compose.js REGISTER_ORDER (suite grew 6 to 8, reds on the unbacked class). No realistic edit produces `color: inherit` on a color-defining register class.

REMEDY (one line, plus one small one)
1. Exclude `inherit` and `currentColor` from what satisfies the `color` assertion.
2. Strip `/* ... */` comments before extracting the rule block.

Red-first: inject `color: inherit` into `.register-nominal`, observe the suite GREEN (the current wrong behavior), then tighten until it reds.

TRIGGER: next time board/web/css/board.css or the register rules are touched, or bundled into the browser-driver work if #33 lands first (a real computed-style assertion would subsume this entirely - `getComputedStyle` resolves `inherit` to the actual inherited value, which is precisely what a text-regex cannot do).
'@
gh issue create --title "Car 31 review MINOR-1: the register-css guard accepts color:inherit, re-creating the #31 defect while passing" --body $b --label "area:view" | Tee-Object -Variable u
gh project item-add 6 --owner polecatspeaks --url $u | Out-Null; Write-Host "on board"

content.text: Real pushback, on three points — one of which corrects my own issue text. Landing it in #33 rather than leaving it in chat.

input.command:
cd C:\Users\Chris\git\starcar
$c = @'
OWNER RULING 2026-07-23: **approved.** Prior-art question answered and CLOSED - there is none to port: the sibling shop is a C# program that uses the Avalonia MCP and on-rig testing, so its UI-verification pattern does not cross to a browser view. Playwright chosen as well-known, mature, and ubiquitous.

The owner invited pushback and coded it a positive outcome. Three refinements follow. None blocks adoption; the third corrects THIS ISSUE'S OWN TEXT, which overstated a capability.

**1. Take the Playwright LIBRARY, not @playwright/test - keep ONE test runner.**
`board/web` currently runs bare `node --test`: no config file, no runner dependency, one command, one CI leg. Adopting `@playwright/test` would add a SECOND runner alongside it - two ways to write a test, two commands, two reporters, two failure formats, and a standing question for every future car about which one to use. The Playwright library API (driving `chromium.launch()` from inside an existing `node --test` file) delivers the whole point of this ticket - a real engine and a real `getComputedStyle` - while keeping one runner and one CI leg. Prefer that unless something concrete demands the runner's fixtures/parallelism, and if it ever does, that is a decision to make on evidence rather than at adoption time.

**2. Screenshot REGENERATION is in scope; screenshot DIFFING is a trap, and this issue blurred them.**
The original text pitched "mechanically regenerate the first-light screenshot" alongside guarding screenshot drift, which reads as an invitation to pixel-diff. It should not. CI runs two legs (windows-latest and ubuntu-latest); font rasterisation and subpixel rendering differ between them, so a pixel-diff assertion would fail across legs for reasons that have nothing to do with the board being wrong. That is the crying-wolf failure mode this shop already ruled worse than no instrument at all (the 54-flags-of-which-50-false scar). SCOPE: regenerate `docs/screenshots/` mechanically so the image cannot silently rot, and assert on COMPUTED VALUES (`getComputedStyle(el).color`), never on pixels. If visual diffing is ever wanted, it needs its own ticket, a single pinned platform, and a tolerance argument.

**3. Sequencing, for the owner to weigh: #32 is a broken existing instrument; this is a new one.**
#32 (the producer hook silently writing no record for 2 of 3 dispatches) is a live Law 1 defect on the shop's own truth surface, and its root cause is not yet established. This ticket adds capability we do not have. A broken instrument arguably outranks a missing one, since everything downstream of a lying instrument is poisoned. Not an argument against doing this - only against doing it FIRST.

**Directionally confirming, though it does not port:** the sibling's "on-rig testing" is the same instinct as this ticket's framing - drive the REAL thing, not a mock. The first spec should serve the real board from the real store, exactly as written.

NIRTS restated with the refinements folded in: ONE spec file, inside the existing `node --test` suite, using the Playwright library, that serves the real board from the real store, reds first on the #31 defect (a nominal row inside a hot lane), asserts computed colour per register, and regenerates the screenshot. No second runner, no pixel diffs, no page objects.
'@
gh issue comment 33 --body $c | Out-Null; Write-Host "commented on #33"

FOUND BY: the Car 31 adversarial reviewer (Opus), round 1, NOTE-1 in artifacts/reviews/2026-07-23-car31-register-css-review-round1-APPROVE.md. Correctly deferred out of #31 because it picks a visual decision reserved to the owner.

MEASURED, not argued. After #31's fix, the reviewer rendered the real snapshot and computed every text-bearing element's effective color. 61 elements still render needs-attention red:

- 54 x `.board-condition` - CORRECT. They carry their own `register-needs-attention` because the server genuinely raised those conditions (`record-unrecognised-fields`, now retired by #26). Honest red. Their repetition is #30's territory, not this one.
- 3 x `.lane-title` + 3 x `.lane-primary` - DESIGN-CONSISTENT. `docs/design/2026-07-23-ui-mockup-brief.md:46` says "A lane's color is the MOST SEVERE of its contributing facts", and these lanes are genuinely stale. A red lane header is the design working.
- 1 x `.track-title` (`board/web/js/dom-writer.js:114`) - THE OPEN EDGE. No CSS rule declares a color for it (`board/web/css/board.css:149-152`), so it inherits the lane's register.
- `.declared-not-observed` (`board/web/js/dom-writer.js:133`) - THE OTHER OPEN EDGE. Has NO CSS rule at all; the reviewer injected one into the fixture and observed it render RED inside a hot lane.

THE QUESTION (owner's call, which is why it is not a patch)
`.track-title` and `.declared-not-observed` are ITEM-LEVEL labels that currently wear the LANE's register. Is that right? A train's title is arguably a property of the train, not of the lane's freshness - in which case it should render paper like the chips inside it. But `.declared-not-observed` is a genuine absence disclosure ("declared, not yet observed") and there is a real argument it SHOULD run hot on its own merits, independent of the lane, since it names a missing thing.

That is two different answers for two elements that today share one accidental behavior. Deciding it means deciding what the register of an item-level label MEANS - which is adjacent to #29 (IDLE is a state) and should probably be ruled on in the same sitting.

REMEDY once ruled: a `color` declaration on `.track-title` and a rule for `.declared-not-observed` in `board/web/css/board.css`. Guarded by the same mechanism as #31 - and if #33 (real-browser driver) lands first, by a computed-style assertion rather than a text regex, which is the honest instrument for an inheritance question.
'@
gh issue create --title "First-light residue: .track-title and .declared-not-observed wear the LANE's register (item-level labels, owner decision)" --body $n1 --label "area:view" | Tee-Object -Variable u1
gh project item-add 6 --owner polecatspeaks --url $u1 | Out-Null

$n2 = @'
FOUND BY: the Car 31 adversarial reviewer (Opus), round 1, NOTE-2 in artifacts/reviews/2026-07-23-car31-register-css-review-round1-APPROVE.md. Law 6 exposure was REDUCED by that commit, not created - this ticket is about finishing the job.

THE REGISTER TAXONOMY (`nominal` / `in-progress` / `needs-attention`) LIVES IN SIX PLACES. The reviewer compared all six mechanically; they ALL AGREE today:

| Mirror | What it holds |
|---|---|
| `schema/yard-snapshot.schema.json` `$defs.register.enum` | the wire's closed set |
| `board/web/js/compose.js:13` `REGISTER_ORDER` | the set AND the severity ordering |
| `board/web/css/board.css` `.register-*` rules | the rendered set |
| `board/assemble/boarddefs.go:29` `closedRegisters` | the Go-side validation gate |
| `board/store/store.go:49` (comment) | documentation of the set |
| `schema/vocab/board-defs.json` | the values actually assigned |

WHAT IS NOW PINNED, AND WHAT IS NOT
Before Car 31's commit, ZERO edges between these six were mechanically pinned - agreement was maintained by hand and by luck. `board/web/test/register-css.test.js` now pins ONE edge: CSS to `compose.js` (proven load-bearing - the reviewer added a 4th register to `REGISTER_ORDER` and the suite grew 6 to 8 and redded on the unbacked class).

STILL UNPINNED (grep finds no test asserting either):
1. `board/web/js/compose.js:13` REGISTER_ORDER  <->  `schema/yard-snapshot.schema.json` `$defs.register.enum`
2. `board/assemble/boarddefs.go:29` closedRegisters  <->  the same enum

Edge 1 is the sharper risk: `compose.js` holds not just the SET but the SEVERITY ORDER, and `mostSevereRegister` (compose.js:23-25) depends on that order being right. The schema enum cannot express ordering, so a drift there would be silent in a way a set-comparison would not catch. State whether the pin covers set-only or set-and-order.

THE EXEMPLAR TO COPY EXISTS IN-REPO: `board/web/test/sse-event-name.test.js` already pins `SSE_EVENT_NAME` against the schema file by reading the real schema rather than restating it. Same shape, same directory, same runner - copy it rather than inventing a pattern. The Go side needs its own equivalent reading `schema/yard-snapshot.schema.json` from disk.

WHY IT IS A NOTE AND NOT A DEFECT: nothing is wrong today, verified mechanically. This is about making a hand-maintained agreement mechanical before it drifts, which is the Healing Loop's "validated facts land as tests or gates, never only prose".

TRIGGER: the next time any register is added, removed, or reordered - or bundled opportunistically into the next `board/` train.
'@
gh issue create --title "Register taxonomy lives in six places; only one edge is mechanically pinned (copy sse-event-name.test.js's pattern)" --body $n2 --label "area:view" | Tee-Object -Variable u2
gh project item-add 6 --owner polecatspeaks --url $u2 | Out-Null

input.command:
cd C:\Users\Chris\git\starcar
$e = @'
NEW EVIDENCE, same session, narrowing the search. Both of this session's completed dispatches have now returned, and the pattern is sharper than the original report:

| Agent | dispatched record | returned record |
|---|---|---|
| Car 31 `addbb80a3b64cbf2e` | ABSENT | ABSENT |
| Car 31's Opus reviewer `ad2b8215ab13183c4` | ABSENT | ABSENT |
| Car 26 `a3bfb5c078bd8da96` | PRESENT (20:43:54Z, commit 0667521) | PRESENT (20:55:53Z, commit 700df05) |

THIS IS A PER-DISPATCH PATTERN, NOT A PER-HOOK ONE. The `dispatched` record is written by the PostToolUse:Task hook (`starcar-producer-launch.sh`) and the `returned` record by the SubagentStop hook (`starcar-producer-stop.sh`) - two different hooks, two different events, ~12 minutes apart. For Car 26 BOTH fired. For the other two NEITHER fired. Whatever the cause is, it selects the DISPATCH, not the hook or the moment.

That materially weakens the original git-index-contention hypothesis: contention would be expected to hit individual hook firings independently (a lock is held for seconds, not for a dispatch's whole lifetime), not to cleanly select two entire dispatches and spare a third. Contention remains possible for the launch hook but cannot easily explain the stop hook failing 12 minutes later for the same two agents and succeeding for the third in the same window.

BETTER-FITTING HYPOTHESES to probe (all UNPROVEN - do not repeat as fact):
1. Something about the dispatch's own shape gates the producer. Car 26 differed from the other two in ways worth testing: it was dispatched while no other agent was running to completion, and it was the only one of the three whose brief was authored after the conductor had done heavy git work. Both are probably coincidental; the point is that a per-dispatch selector exists and the shape of the dispatch is where to look.
2. CONCURRENCY: Car 31's reviewer and Car 26 were running SIMULTANEOUSLY. Car 26 was dispatched (20:43:54) while the reviewer was mid-flight, and both stopped within ~40 seconds of each other. If the producer serialises on something per-repo, a second concurrent dispatch could be silently dropped - but note the SURVIVOR here is the later one, which is the opposite of a naive first-wins lock.
3. The hook's own non-fatal posture swallows the evidence: `starcar-producer-launch.sh` exits 0 when pwsh is absent, by design, so any other silent failure path is equally invisible. There is currently NO log of hook invocations, so we cannot distinguish "hook never fired" from "hook fired and failed".

WHAT TO DO FIRST, and it is not a fix
Instrument before theorising. Add an append-only invocation log to both producer hooks (timestamp, agent id, event, exit path) so the next occurrence distinguishes never-fired from fired-and-failed. Then dispatch repeatedly, including concurrently, and OBSERVE. This is a behavioural claim and the probe doctrine forbids settling it by reading.

BACKFILL PATH CONFIRMED WORKING (the silver lining): `scripts/Land-Verdict.ps1` exists precisely for "backfill happens precisely when no producer hook fired" and it recovered the reviewer's verdict from the parent session transcript - landed verbatim, 25941 chars, sha256 aef0aeb1..., and `Verify-Verdict.ps1` now reports 50/50 bodies matching their hashes. So no verdict was LOST to this defect; it just was not automatic.

ONE USABILITY TRAP FOUND WHILE DOING THAT BACKFILL, worth its own fix: Land-Verdict.ps1 wants the PARENT session transcript (`~/.claude/projects/<project>/<session-id>.jsonl`), because the `<result>` block lives there. Pointing it at the obvious-looking per-task `.output` file (the subagent's own JSONL) fails with "No result block found for task id X. A dispatch that never completed has no verdict to land." That message names the WRONG CAUSE - the dispatch completed fine; the operator supplied the wrong transcript. A misleading error on a recovery tool is expensive exactly when it is needed. Suggest the message distinguish "no result block in this file" from "dispatch never completed", and name the expected parent-transcript path.
'@

# RESUME HERE — checkpoint 2026-07-23, ~16:50 (rewritten mid-session)

**This checkpoint is written CONTINUOUSLY, not at session end.** A session can die
without warning (it did today: a credit limit killed session `67913b32` mid-work at
~16:21). A checkpoint that is only written at a graceful close does not exist when the
close is not graceful. Update this file the moment state changes — especially on dispatch.

  yet, NOT merged.
- `dev` carries `0667521` (producer auto-commit) — check ahead/behind before assuming.

## THE LIVE DEFECT FOUND THIS SESSION (highest-value open thread)
**The producer hook silently skipped 2 of this session's 3 dispatches.** Only Car 26
(`a3bfb5c078bd8da96`) got a `dispatched` record; Car 31 and its reviewer got none, and
`git status` is clean so they were never written (not written-and-uncommitted, which is
the OTHER known failure mode — see `1182e09`). All day before this the producer was
regular: 20 dispatched records, ~10:14 through 15:00.

**Why this is structural, not cosmetic:** the harness detects a missing artifact by
finding a `dispatched` record with no matching `returned` one. A dispatch that never
writes a `dispatched` record is not overdue — it is INVISIBLE. The mechanism built to
make a killed session survivable has a blind spot exactly where the kill happens.
Root cause NOT yet established (hypothesis, unproven: git index contention with the
conductor's own concurrent heavy git operations right after dispatch). Do not assert a
cause without probing it.

## What landed earlier today (unchanged, still true)
The yard-board train (#1) is COMPLETE and FIRST-LIT: `board/` (Go server + vanilla-ESM
view), the fold, the producer budget stamp, four CI legs watched red AND green, README
quickstart verified from clean clones twice. Board rendered the real store and generated
its own backlog in its first minute. Story: `docs/retros/2026-07-23-board-train-retro.md`.
Also: #20 gate scoping, doc map + glossary, house vocabulary (NIRTS, HAVAGLANCE, FULL
SENTENCE FLOW, ask-don't-infer), mocks-as-direction, #22 effort/budget ruling.

- Housekeeping done: 18 open issues given their missing area label; 37 stale worktrees
  and 11 fully-merged `car/*` branches pruned.
- Go is NOT on PATH in either tool shell — full path `C:\Program Files\Go\bin\go.exe`.
  `sh` is absent from the pwsh tool's PATH (probe suites red there; known env gap).
